guidance.cx-action-area:
Use action area when a complete content block needs contextual actions without changing the block's normal layout. It is for read views where the user should act on the exact thing they are looking at, such as editing a paragraph, opening information for a section, or asking an assistant to work with one block.

Use info, askAi, and editable for the standard block actions. They render as one right-aligned action group in this order: Info, Ask AI, custom actions, Edit. Use actions only for extra custom actions that belong to the same block.

The action group should stay on one line. When it no longer fits, it first hides shortcut hints from every button. If it still does not fit, buttons with icons become icon-only buttons. Keep actions local to the wrapped content. Do not use action area as a page toolbar, a card replacement, or a way to hide primary actions. Shortcuts should only trigger while the area is hovered or focused, and each visible shortcut must actually work in that context.
guidance.cx-alert:
Use alerts for contextual feedback that explains a state the user needs to notice and understand. Do not default to alerts: if the message has no urgency and does not need extra attention, use normal body text instead. Alerts are not for ordinary descriptive copy. Use mood to describe the message, from light emphasis to stronger intervention: default when information only needs to stand out a little more than body text, info when informational context is more important to see, success for confirmation, warning for preventable risk, and danger for failure or a blocked action.

Alerts should be short, actionable, and calm. They are not banners, toasts, or field validation. They sit on the canvas, close to the thing they explain. Add an action only when the next useful step can happen directly from the alert, and make an alert dismissible only when the user can safely ignore it after reading.
guidance.cx-badge:
Use badges as compact attention markers. The default badge should usually appear without a count: it says there is something worth noticing, not how much work is waiting. This is the normal choice for filters, controls, navigation items, or places where the user only needs a small visual signal.

Add a count only when the number itself helps the user act, such as unread notifications, events, selected items, or active filters. Do not add a number just because the component supports one. Most badges should stay without a count.
guidance.cx-banner:
Use banners only when the message must interrupt the page and stay impossible to miss. They are for rare, high-importance information: a major system state, a critical warning, or a success message the user truly needs to see before moving on.

Do not use banners for temporary errors, routine confirmations, or lightweight feedback. A banner takes over the top of the interface, so it should carry something worth that interruption. Keep the copy short, direct, and specific, and include an action only when it helps the user respond immediately.
guidance.cx-avatar:
Use avatars to identify a person, account, team, or system at a glance. Prefer an image when it is stable and recognizable. Otherwise, use the name so the initials stay consistent and readable.

Avatars should support recognition, not decoration. Keep the color automatic unless the surrounding product already has a meaningful color choice. Use the badge only as a small attention marker, and let it keep the default badge color so identity and status do not compete.
guidance.cx-breadcrumbs:
Use breadcrumbs when the user is inside a real hierarchy and needs to understand where the current page lives. They are not a replacement for back, and they should not appear on shallow pages just to fill space.

Keep the first and current level visible. When the path is too deep or the space is too narrow, collapse the middle levels into the hidden-level menu. Use switchable crumbs only when the user can stay in the same kind of view while changing a scope, source, customer, or other level in the hierarchy.
guidance.cx-budget:
Use budgets to show how a value relates to a known limit. They work best when the user needs to understand pace, remaining capacity, or whether usage is getting close to a boundary. Use percent when the exact unit is not important, currency when the amount itself changes the decision, and unit for things like seats, credits, hours, or storage.

Use the compact variant when the budget is a small supporting signal. Use the detailed variant when both the current value and the total need to be visible. Favor describes what good looks like: low when a smaller value is better, high when a larger value is better. Add a hint only when the extra context helps the user act, such as expiry, renewal, or reserved budget.
guidance.cx-button:
Use the default button for almost every action. When in doubt, use default. Primary is only for the action that moves the user toward their goal, and it should usually appear once per view or once inside the element it belongs to.

Use danger for destructive actions, and make the surrounding flow add enough friction before something is lost. Use transparent when the action should stay quiet inside dense UI, toolbars, menus, or supporting controls. Use rounded only when a pill shape is intentional. Button text should name the action, not the state: "Delete file" beats "Confirm". Icon-only buttons need a clear ariaLabel.
guidance.cx-card:
Use cards to group one self-contained piece of content. The default card should stay light: border only, padded content, and no header unless the group needs a name.

Add a background only when the card needs more separation from the page. Add a header when the card needs a title, icon, status, local menu, or meta content. Use the meta slot for small supporting status such as a tag. Use menuItems only for actions that belong to that card. Set interactive only when the whole card is a real target, such as opening a detail view. Remove padding only when the content needs to sit flush, such as tables, charts, media, or custom layouts.
guidance.cx-code-field:
Use code fields only for short one-time verification codes. They are for moments where the user is copying a six-character code from email, SMS, an authenticator app, or a recovery flow.

Do not use a code field for passwords, serial numbers, IDs, invite codes, API keys, or long tokens. Those need normal text fields because the user may need to read, select, edit, or paste the value as one piece. Keep validation close to the field, and let paste work generously.
guidance.cx-split-button:
Use split buttons only when one action has a clear default and the menu contains close variations of that same action. The main side runs the default action; the chevron opens related alternatives.

Do not use a split button for unrelated actions or as a way to hide decisions the user must make first. If there is no obvious default action, use a menu button instead. Keep labels direct, keep destructive alternatives visibly dangerous, and use primary only when the default action is the main path forward.
guidance.cx-variant:
Use variants to show realistic examples of how a component can be used. Each tab should represent a meaningful scenario, state, or composition that helps someone understand the component in context, not just a prop value in isolation.

Variants are not a full workbench and should not try to expose every prop. Use them when the useful question is "what does this look like in real product use?" Keep the names short, choose examples people would actually build, and let the specimen do the explaining.
guidance.cx-big-checkbox:
Use a big checkbox when one choice needs more room than a small checkbox can give it. It works well for permissions, setup choices, and options where the label needs a short explanation.

Keep the copy short and make the whole item easy to scan. If the choice is part of a long form or a dense table, use a regular checkbox instead.
guidance.cx-big-radio:
Use a big radio when the user must choose one clear option from a small set. It is best for choices that need a title, a short explanation, or a more touch-friendly target.

Keep the options parallel so they are easy to compare. If the choice is simple and space is tight, use regular radios instead.
guidance.cx-button-group:
Use a button group for a small set of closely related choices where one choice is active at a time. It should feel faster than a select and clearer than several separate buttons.

Keep labels short and make each option the same kind of decision. If there are many options or the labels become long, use a select instead.
guidance.cx-calendar:
Use a calendar when the date itself matters in relation to nearby days. It is useful for planning, scheduling, and choosing dates where weekday context helps.

Do not use it when the user only needs to enter a known date quickly. In that case, a date field is quieter and faster.
guidance.cx-chart:
Use charts to show shape, trend, comparison, or proportion. A chart should answer a real question faster than a table would.

Keep the chart focused. If the user needs exact values, pair it with a table or use a simpler metric instead.
guidance.cx-checkbox:
Use a checkbox for an independent on/off choice. It should describe a state that can be true or false without changing the meaning of nearby controls.

Write the label as the thing being enabled. Avoid negative labels, because they make checked and unchecked states harder to reason about.
guidance.cx-code-block:
Use a code block when the user needs to read or copy code, commands, logs, or structured text. It should preserve spacing and make the content feel separate from body copy.

Keep surrounding explanation short. If the code is only a tiny value, use inline code or a snippet instead.
guidance.cx-color-picker:
Use a color picker when choosing the color is the actual task. It is useful for theme settings, labels, charts, and visual customization.

Prefer named or token-based choices when consistency matters. Free color choice should be rare in product UI.
guidance.cx-context-dialog:
Use a context dialog for a small decision that belongs to the thing the user just interacted with. It should feel local, quick, and specific.

Do not use it for heavy workflows or important confirmations. If the decision affects the whole page or needs more space, use a dialog.
guidance.cx-date-picker:
Use a date picker when the user needs one specific date. It should make choosing a nearby date easy while still allowing direct entry when the date is known.

Keep helper text close to the field only when it prevents mistakes. Do not add extra explanation for obvious dates.
guidance.cx-date-span-picker:
Use a date span picker when the user needs a start and end date that belong together. It is best for reports, filters, bookings, and time windows.

Make the selected range easy to understand at a glance. If the range has limits or consequences, say that before the user commits.
guidance.cx-dialog:
Use a dialog when the user must make a focused decision without losing the current page. It should be reserved for important choices, short forms, or confirmations that need attention.

Keep the dialog small and purposeful. If the content grows into a workflow, it probably belongs on its own page or in a fullscreen dialog.
guidance.cx-divider:
Use a divider to separate groups that need a visible pause. It should help the eye understand structure, not decorate the page.

If spacing alone is enough, skip the divider. Too many lines make an interface feel heavier than it is.
guidance.cx-validation-message:
Use a validation message to explain what is wrong and how to fix it. The message should appear close to the field or action it belongs to.

Write it in plain language. Do not blame the user, and do not show validation before the user has had a fair chance to act.
guidance.cx-expansion-panel:
Use an expansion panel for secondary information that is useful but not always needed. It helps keep the page calm while still making details easy to reach.

The heading must clearly promise what will open. Avoid hiding critical information inside a collapsed panel.
guidance.cx-file-upload:
Use file upload when the user is adding a file as part of a task. The control should make accepted file types, limits, and current file state easy to understand.

Do not make the user guess what happened after selecting a file. Show the selected file, upload progress, or the next required action.
guidance.cx-fullscreen-dialog:
Use a fullscreen dialog for focused work that needs more space but should still feel temporary. It is useful for editors, setup flows, and detailed review tasks.

Make closing and saving behavior obvious. If the user is building something important, protect them from losing work.
guidance.cx-grid:
Use a grid when items need to line up in columns and respond cleanly to available space. It is best for cards, metrics, and repeated content blocks.

The grid should serve the content, not force it. If the items need to be read in a strict order, a list may be clearer.
guidance.cx-hierarchical-select:
Use a hierarchical select when choices are nested and the path matters. It helps users move through categories without flattening everything into one long list.

Keep the hierarchy shallow when possible. If users need to compare many branches at once, a tree view may be better.
guidance.cx-icon:
Use icons to support recognition, status, or compact actions. An icon should clarify the interface, not replace words when the meaning could be unclear.

Keep icon use consistent. If an icon needs explanation every time, it probably needs text or a tooltip.
guidance.cx-icon-button:
Use an icon button for compact actions where the icon is already familiar or the space is constrained. It is best for repeated tools and secondary actions.

Always make the accessible name specific. If the action is important or unfamiliar, use a text button instead.
guidance.cx-image:
Use an image when the user benefits from seeing the actual thing. It should support recognition, inspection, or context.

Avoid decorative images in product UI unless they clearly improve comprehension. Empty or broken image states should still feel intentional.
guidance.cx-inline:
Use inline layout for a small row of related items. It keeps actions, labels, and small metadata aligned without creating a new visual block.

Do not use it for content that needs to wrap into paragraphs or compare across rows. When the row gets crowded, move to a stack or grid.
guidance.cx-text-field:
Use a text field for short free text. The label should name the value the user is entering, and the field should stay close to its label.

Do not use it for long notes, rich text, or values with strict formats unless the formatting help is clear.
guidance.cx-lightbox:
Use a lightbox when the user needs to inspect media without leaving the current page. It should feel like a temporary closer look.

Keep controls minimal and predictable. If the media needs editing or a complex workflow, use a dedicated view instead.
guidance.cx-list-view:
Use a list view for repeated items that need to be scanned vertically. It is good for navigation, search results, and compact summaries.

Make each row readable without forcing the user to open it. Put the most important information first.
guidance.cx-loader:
Use a loader when content is coming and the wait is short enough that the user should stay in place. It tells the user the system is working.

Prefer skeletons or preserved layout when the shape of the content is known. Do not use loaders to hide slow or unclear states.
guidance.cx-log:
Use a log to show a sequence of system events or process steps. It should help the user understand what happened and what may need attention.

Keep entries short and ordered. If the user needs to act on an entry, make the action easy to find.
guidance.cx-log-step:
Use a log step for one item in a process history. The status, label, and time should quickly explain where the process stands.

Do not overload each step with prose. A log is for scanning first, detail second.
guidance.cx-markdown:
Use markdown rendering for trusted, formatted content that needs simple structure. It is useful for notes, guidance, and generated summaries.

Keep markdown restrained in product UI. Too many heading levels, links, or styles can make the page feel unmanaged.
guidance.cx-menu:
Use a menu for a short list of contextual actions. It should appear where the user expects the actions to belong.

Do not use a menu to hide primary actions. If something is the main next step, it should be visible.
guidance.cx-menu-header:
Use a menu header to name a group of actions when the grouping is not obvious. It should help the user scan, not create decoration.

Keep headers short. If every group needs a long explanation, the menu is doing too much.
guidance.cx-menu-label:
Use a menu label for small supporting text inside a menu. It can explain state, scope, or a group of actions.

Keep it quiet and brief. Menu labels should never compete with the actions themselves.
guidance.cx-metric:
Use a metric for one important number and its label. It should help the user understand status quickly.

Do not pack too many metrics together. A metric loses power when everything on the page is shouting.
guidance.cx-option:
Use an option for one selectable item inside a choice list. The label should make the choice understandable without extra context.

Use supporting text only when it helps compare options. Avoid repeating the same words across every option.
guidance.cx-option-group:
Use an option group when related choices need a shared label or structure. It helps break a larger choice into understandable parts.

Keep groups meaningful. If the grouping does not change how the user decides, it adds friction.
guidance.cx-pagination:
Use pagination when there are more items than should be shown at once and the user benefits from stable pages. It works well for lists that are browsed or revisited.

Do not paginate tiny lists. If the user is likely to search or filter instead, make those controls strong.
guidance.cx-number-field:
Use a number field when the user must enter an exact numeric value. It should make units, limits, and invalid values clear.

Use a slider only when approximate adjustment is the main task. Exact values belong in a number field.
guidance.cx-placeholder:
Use a placeholder to reserve space for content that is missing, loading, or not configured yet. It should explain the empty state without feeling broken.

Do not use placeholders as decoration. They should either help the user wait, understand, or act.
guidance.cx-progress-bar:
Use a progress bar when completion has a clear start and end. It should help the user understand how far along a task, quota, or process is.

Avoid fake precision. If progress cannot be measured honestly, use a status message or loader instead.
guidance.cx-qr-code:
Use a QR code when scanning is genuinely faster than typing or copying. It is useful for device pairing, login handoff, and sharing links across devices.

Always provide a fallback. A QR code should never be the only way to complete an important task.
guidance.cx-query-element:
Use a query element to show one part of a structured filter or query. It should make the condition readable as a small building block.

Keep labels close to the user language. The user should not need to understand implementation terms to read the query.
guidance.cx-query-field:
Use a query field when the user builds or edits a structured search. It is best when filters need to be visible, editable, and combined.

Do not make simple search feel like programming. Use the query field only when structure gives the user real control.
guidance.cx-radio:
Use radios when the user must choose exactly one option from a small set. All options should be visible so the user can compare them.

Avoid using radios for many items or very long labels. A select or searchable control will be easier to scan.
guidance.cx-select:
Use a select for choosing one value from a larger or less frequently changed set. It keeps the page calm when showing all options would be too much.

Use clear labels and sensible defaults. If users need to compare options side by side, radios or a button group may be better.
guidance.cx-secret-display:
Use secret display for sensitive values that must be revealed, copied, or inspected with care. The default state should protect the value.

Make reveal and copy actions deliberate. Do not expose secrets just because there is enough space.
guidance.cx-search-field:
Use a search field when the user is narrowing a collection by typing. It should be easy to find, quick to clear, and forgiving of partial input.

Pair search with good empty states. A no-results state should help the user adjust, not leave them stuck.
guidance.cx-popover:
Use a popover for small supporting content or controls that belong to a trigger. It should be quick to open and quick to leave.

Do not put long workflows in a popover. If the user needs to think, compare, or save work, use a larger surface.
guidance.cx-severity-tag:
Use a severity tag to show how serious something is. It helps users spot risk, urgency, or impact quickly.

Use severity sparingly and consistently. If everything is severe, nothing is.
guidance.cx-shortcut-key:
Use shortcut keys to show keyboard commands in a compact, recognizable way. They are helpful near menus, toolbars, and command references.

Only show shortcuts that actually work in that context. Wrong shortcut hints erode trust fast.
guidance.cx-slider:
Use a slider when the user is choosing a value by feel within a known range. It works best for approximate settings such as intensity, size, or volume.

Do not use a slider when the exact number matters. Give the user a number field or another exact control instead.
guidance.cx-snippet:
Use a snippet for short copyable text such as tokens, paths, commands, or IDs. It should make copying easier without turning the page into a code block.

Keep the snippet compact and readable. If the content wraps heavily, use a code block instead.
guidance.cx-spinner:
Use a spinner for very small loading states where preserving layout is not important. It should signal that a single control or small area is busy.

Avoid using spinners as the main page state. Larger waits need clearer feedback.
guidance.cx-split:
Use split layout when two areas need to sit beside each other with a clear relationship. It is useful for master-detail, summary-detail, and preview-edit patterns.

Keep the relationship obvious. If the two sides are unrelated, use separate sections instead.
guidance.cx-stack:
Use stack layout when content should flow vertically with consistent rhythm. It is the default layout choice for forms, settings, and grouped content.

Let the stack carry spacing instead of adding one-off margins. This keeps pages easier to maintain.
guidance.cx-sso-button:
Use an SSO button for signing in with a trusted provider. The provider name should be clear and the button should not look like a generic action.

Only show providers that are actually available. Authentication choices should feel reliable, not experimental.
guidance.cx-status-tag:
Use a status tag to show the current state of an item or process. It should help users scan lists and understand what needs attention.

Keep statuses stable and plain. Avoid clever labels that make state harder to interpret.
guidance.cx-switch:
Use a switch for a setting that takes effect as on or off. It is best for preferences, visibility, and feature toggles.

Use a checkbox instead when the choice is part of a form submission or the user is selecting items from a list.
guidance.cx-table:
Use a table when users need to compare rows and columns. It should make scanning, sorting, and finding patterns easier than cards or freeform layout would.

Put the most important columns first and keep cell content concise. If the row needs a story, it probably is not a table row.
guidance.cx-tabs:
Use tabs to switch between sibling views at the same level. Tabs should keep the user in place while changing the content below.

Keep labels short and stable. If there are too many tabs to scan, use overflow behavior rather than squeezing every label into view.
guidance.cx-tag:
Use a tag for compact metadata, categories, or applied filters. It should add context without becoming the main content.

Do not use tags as decorations. Each tag should help the user understand or act.
guidance.cx-tag-input:
Use tag input when the user can add multiple short values. It is useful for labels, recipients, filters, and keywords.

Make duplicate, invalid, and removable states clear. The user should always know what has been added.
guidance.cx-steps:
Use steps when a process has a known sequence. It helps the user understand progress, current position, and what comes next.

Do not use steps for loose navigation. The order should matter.
guidance.cx-textarea:
Use a textarea for longer free text such as notes, descriptions, and comments. It gives the user room to think and revise.

Do not use it for short values that should stay scannable in a form. A text field is cleaner for those.
guidance.cx-time-input:
Use time input when the user needs a specific time. It should make the expected format and any limits clear.

Pair it with a date when the day matters. Time without context can become ambiguous fast.
guidance.cx-toast:
Use a toast for short feedback after an action. It should confirm, warn, or explain something lightweight without interrupting the user.

Do not use toasts for critical information or anything the user must read later. Important messages need a persistent place.
guidance.cx-toggle-button:
Use a toggle button for an action that can stay on or off, especially in toolbars. The pressed state should be visually obvious.

Do not use it for navigation. If the user is switching views, tabs are clearer.
guidance.cx-toggle-chip:
Use a toggle chip for compact filter choices that can be turned on or off. It works well when several chips can sit together.

Keep chip labels short. If the choice needs explanation, use a larger control.
guidance.cx-toggle-chip-group:
Use a toggle chip group for a set of compact filters or modes. It should help users scan available choices and see what is active.

Do not use it for high-stakes choices. Chips are lightweight by nature.
guidance.cx-tooltip:
Use a tooltip for short help that explains an icon, label, or small control. It should clarify something the user is already near.

Do not hide essential information in a tooltip. Tooltips are support, not the main interface.
guidance.cx-tree-view:
Use a tree view for nested structures where the user needs to expand, collapse, and understand hierarchy. It is useful for folders, scopes, and grouped navigation.

Keep the tree readable. If the hierarchy is too deep, add search or a simpler way to jump.
guidance.cx-trend-tag:
Use a trend tag to show direction or change in a compact way. It works best beside a metric or list item where the comparison matters.

Make the baseline clear. A trend without context can look meaningful while saying very little.
